1.synthesis:
Psalm 72 bears the superscription 'A Psalm of Solomon' — either composed by Solomon or for him — and closes Book II of the Psalter (Ps 42–72). Verse 20 — 'the prayers of David, the son of Jesse, are ended' — serves as the editorial closure of the Davidic collection within Book II. The psalm is a royal prayer for the king's justice, and its language escalates from historical prayer to eschatological vision. The opening petition — 'O God, give the king your justice, and your righteousness to the king's son' (v. 1) — asks that the king govern with the very attributes of YHWH himself: divine justice and righteousness delegated to the human ruler. This is the theology of kingship in ancient Israel: the king was not autonomous but the agent of YHWH's rule, his justice an extension of the divine justice.

The primary objects of royal justice are stated in vv. 2–4: 'your people with righteousness and your poor with justice.' In ancient Israelite royal ideology (and throughout the ANE), the king's primary obligation was the protection of the poor, widow, and orphan from exploitation by the powerful. Hammurabi's law code opens with the claim that Marduk commissioned him to ensure the strong do not oppress the weak. The cosmic dimension of righteous governance appears in v. 3 — 'may the mountains bring peace to the people, and the hills, through righteousness' — the covenant blessing of Lev 26:4–6 expressed in royal theology: just kingship produces agricultural fertility and peace in the natural order.
1.historical_context: Solomon's reign is the historical context — the prayer for the king's just rule under which the nation flourished initially (1 Kgs 3:28; 4:20–25) before deteriorating. The longevity petition of v. 5 — 'may he live while the sun endures, and as long as the moon, throughout all generations' — escalates beyond any realistic human lifespan into the eschatological, suggesting that even in its original form the psalm reached beyond the historical Solomon toward the ideal king. The 'king's son' (v. 1) receiving YHWH's righteousness reflects the Davidic covenant's dynastic promise: the divine blessing transfers through the Davidic lineage.
1.literary_structure: The first section (vv. 1–7) focuses on the king's internal governance: his relationship to the poor and the natural order. The movement from justice for the poor (vv. 2–4) through longevity prayer (v. 5) to natural order flourishing (vv. 6–7) — the king like rain on mown grass, like showers watering the earth — creates a vision of royal rule as the condition for cosmic and social flourishing. The rainfall imagery of v. 6 connects righteous kingship to agricultural provision: the king who rules justly makes the rain fall, figuratively and perhaps literally through divine blessing.
1.theological_application: The royal ideology of Psalm 72 defines the measure of legitimate authority by its treatment of the most vulnerable: justice for the poor is not an optional program of the just king but his defining obligation. This criterion of governance — does it protect or exploit the vulnerable? — is the consistent standard by which the prophets evaluate Israel's kings and the consistent standard by which all human governance is measured in the OT. The church's political theology draws on this criterion: the most vulnerable members of society are the measure of a government's justice.
1.christology: The psalm's language escalates beyond any historical Solomon to the Messiah: longevity surpassing sun and moon (v. 5), worldwide dominion (v. 8), universal worship (v. 11), permanent covenant prayer (v. 15). The NT reads Psalm 72 messianically: the Magi's gifts (Matt 2:11) echo vv. 10–11. Jesus, the Son of David who definitively fulfills the royal obligation of protecting the poor ('the Spirit of the Lord has anointed me to bring good news to the poor,' Luke 4:18), is the king whose justice makes the mountains bring peace and whose reign endures as long as the sun.

8.synthesis:
The second section of Psalm 72 escalates from national justice to universal dominion: 'May he have dominion from sea to sea, and from the River to the ends of the earth!' (v. 8). This verse has shaped the geography of messianic expectation — the 'sea to sea' and 'River to the ends of the earth' coordinates define the scope of the ideal king's rule as the whole earth. The submission of enemies (v. 9: 'may his foes bow down before him, and his enemies lick the dust') and the tribute of international kings (vv. 10–11: 'the kings of Tarshish and of the coastlands render him tribute; the kings of Sheba and Seba bring gifts; all kings fall down before him, all nations serve him') paint a picture of universal political submission under the ideal king's reign.

The geographical scope — Tarshish (far west, Spain or beyond), Sheba and Seba (Arabian south, Ethiopia) — represents the breadth of the known world. The kings mentioned are from the extremities of the oikoumene, not merely Israel's neighbors. The tribute brought is both economic (gold, gifts) and political (submission, service) — the full expression of international covenant relationship. This vision of universal royal submission echoes Solomon's historical court (1 Kgs 10:23–25) but exceeds it in scope and permanence, pointing toward a reign that no historical king has yet achieved.
8.historical_context: The 'River' (nāhār) of v. 8 is typically the Euphrates — the eastern boundary of Solomon's sphere of influence at its greatest extent (1 Kgs 4:21). 'From sea to sea' may refer to Mediterranean to Red Sea (the historical borders of the promised land) or to a more cosmological framing of the entire earth between its bounding seas. Tarshish (v. 10) appears in trade routes in 1 Kgs 10:22 and is associated with the far west; Sheba is the southwestern Arabian kingdom famous for the Queen of Sheba's visit to Solomon (1 Kgs 10:1–13). The Magi of Matt 2 come from the east bearing gold and gifts — a partial fulfillment of this eschatological vision.
8.literary_structure: The section moves from geographical scope (v. 8) to enemy submission (v. 9) to international tribute (vv. 10–11). The escalation from the local poor (vv. 2–4) to the global kings (vv. 10–11) is deliberate: the king who protects the poorest of his subjects will also command the submission of the mightiest foreign powers. Justice toward the weak and dominion over the strong are not competing but correlative aspects of the ideal royal reign.
8.theological_application: The universal dominion of the ideal king in Psalm 72 establishes the OT's expectation that divine kingship will ultimately encompass the whole earth — not merely Israel's territory. This eschatological universalism drives the prophetic vision of all nations streaming to Zion and all kings submitting to Israel's God. It is the theological ground for the NT's Great Commission: the king whose dominion extends 'to the ends of the earth' (v. 8) sends his servants to all nations to claim what is already his.
8.christology: 'From sea to sea, and from the River to the ends of the earth' (v. 8) is the territorial claim that underlies 'all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me' (Matt 28:18). Christ's universal authority is the fulfillment of the ideal king's worldwide dominion. The submission of all kings (v. 11) anticipates 'every knee will bow' (Phil 2:10) and the vision of Rev 21:24: 'the kings of the earth will bring their glory into it' — the eschatological Zion receiving the tribute of all nations under the perfect royal authority of the Lamb.

12.synthesis:
The third section returns to the psalm's core social theology — justice for the poor — and grounds it in the most concrete description of the king's active protective role: 'For he delivers the needy when he calls, the poor and him who has no helper. He has pity on the weak and the needy, and saves the lives of the needy. From oppression and violence he redeems their life, and precious is their blood in his sight' (vv. 12–14). The four-fold characterization — needy when he calls, poor with no helper, weak and needy, those under oppression and violence — is the most comprehensive portrait of the protected class in the Psalter. The declaration that 'precious is their blood in his sight' (v. 14) is the theological ground: the blood of the vulnerable has covenant weight before YHWH, demanding the king's active protection.

The covenant-blessing vision of vv. 15–17 — gold of Sheba brought to him, prayer for him continually, grain abundant on the tops of mountains, the name of the king enduring forever and being blessed through all nations — restates the eschatological scope of the first two sections through the lens of the Abrahamic promise: 'may all nations be blessed through him' (v. 17). The ideal king is explicitly placed in the line of Abrahamic blessing: what YHWH promised Abraham is fulfilled through the king's just reign.
12.historical_context: 'Precious is their blood in his sight' (v. 14) — the covenant significance of blood: to shed the blood of the innocent was to incur covenant guilt that demanded reckoning. The king who regards the blood of the poor as 'precious' (yāqār — the word used for precious stones and rare commodities) treats the vulnerable's life as having the highest value, countering the economic calculus that treats the poor as disposable. The grain-on-mountain-tops prosperity of v. 16 — 'may there be abundance of grain in the land; on the tops of the mountains may it wave' — is the covenant blessing of Lev 26:4–5 expressed in hyperbolic terms: grain so abundant it grows on the peaks. The 'blessed through him' formula of v. 17 directly echoes Gen 12:3 — the Abrahamic blessing now mediated through the Davidic king.
12.literary_structure: The section's structure is: protective action toward the poor (vv. 12–14), followed by the covenant blessings that flow from just rule (vv. 15–17). The connection is causal: because the king protects the blood of the poor (vv. 12–14), the gold of Sheba comes (v. 15), the grain flourishes (v. 16), and the name endures forever (v. 17). Justice toward the vulnerable produces both natural and national prosperity — the same correlation as the covenant blessing system of Deuteronomy, now concentrated in the person of the just king.
12.theological_application: The theology of v. 14 — 'precious is their blood in his sight' — is one of the OT's strongest statements of the intrinsic value of the marginalized. The king's justice is measured not by the power he commands but by the protection he provides to those who cannot protect themselves. This criterion — the treatment of the most vulnerable — is the covenant measure of all human authority. James applies this logic to the church: 'religion that God our Father accepts as pure and faultless is this: to look after orphans and widows in their distress' (Jas 1:27).
12.christology: The Abrahamic blessing mediated through the king — 'all nations shall be blessed through him' (v. 17) — is explicitly applied to Christ in Gal 3:8–9: 'Scripture foresaw that God would justify the Gentiles by faith, and announced the gospel in advance to Abraham: “All nations will be blessed through you.”' Christ is the David's Son through whom the Abrahamic blessing reaches all nations. The 'precious blood' of v. 14 anticipates the 'precious blood of Christ' (1 Pet 1:19): the one who regarded the blood of the poor as precious himself shed his blood — the most precious — to redeem all who are oppressed and without helper.

18.synthesis:
The final three verses of Psalm 72 are not strictly part of the psalm itself but serve as the editorial doxology closing Book II of the Psalter (Ps 41:13 closes Book I, Ps 89:52 closes Book III, Ps 106:48 closes Book IV): 'Blessed be the LORD, the God of Israel, who alone does wondrous things. Blessed be his glorious name forever; may the whole earth be filled with his glory! Amen and Amen' (vv. 18–19). The editorial doxology places the entire royal prayer of Psalm 72 within the frame of YHWH's own glory — the king's just reign is ultimately a vehicle for YHWH's glory filling the whole earth. The prayer 'may the whole earth be filled with his glory' echoes the seraphic liturgy of Isa 6:3 and becomes the eschatological hope toward which the ideal king's worldwide dominion points.

The editorial note of v. 20 — 'The prayers of David, the son of Jesse, are ended' — marks the closure of the Davidic collection within Book II. This note is somewhat misleading since there are many Davidic psalms in Books III–V; it probably marks the closure of a specific early Davidic collection that was integrated into the larger Psalter. The double 'Amen' (ʾāmēn wĕʾāmēn) of v. 19 is the congregation's liturgical seal on the doxology — affirming that what has been prayed and proclaimed is true and settled.
18.historical_context: The five-book structure of the Psalter is modeled on the Pentateuch, with editorial doxologies closing each book and marking the canonical boundaries of the collection. Book II (Pss 42–72) is predominantly Elohistic (using Elohim for the divine name) and dominated by the Asaph and Sons of Korah collections alongside Davidic psalms. The closing doxology's declaration that YHWH 'alone does wondrous things' (v. 18) is the monotheistic claim of the entire Psalter: the wondrous acts performed through the ideal king are YHWH's own acts, mediated through the covenant relationship.
18.literary_structure: The doxology functions as a frame for the entire book: Book II's prayers — including the Elohistic psalms of lament, praise, and royal theology — are all encompassed in the praise of YHWH who alone does wondrous things. The eschatological petition — 'may the whole earth be filled with his glory' — is the goal toward which all the book's prayers tend: the individual laments, the royal psalms, the Asaph wisdom-psalms are all movements within the larger trajectory toward YHWH's universal glory. The 'Amen and Amen' is the congregation's acknowledgment that this goal, though not yet visible, is the true end of the story.
18.theological_application: The doxology's framing of the king's justice within YHWH's glory establishes the theological principle that human authority — even at its most just and far-reaching — is penultimate to divine glory. The king's universal dominion is the vehicle for the earth being filled with YHWH's glory, not an end in itself. All human governance, justice, and authority derive their meaning and legitimacy from their relationship to this eschatological goal. The church's political engagement is shaped by this frame: pursuing justice is pursuing the conditions under which YHWH's glory may be known in the earth.
18.christology: 'May the whole earth be filled with his glory!' (v. 19) is the prayer that the NT announces as answered in Christ: 'the earth will be filled with the knowledge of the glory of God as the waters cover the sea' (Hab 2:14) is fulfilled through the gospel's proclamation in all the earth. The Father's purpose in exalting Christ — 'that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow... and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father' (Phil 2:10–11) — is the specific content of the Psalter's 'Amen and Amen': the universal glorification of YHWH through the exaltation of his King.
